nodes.name: Beer & Brewing
nodes.description: Beer styles, famous breweries, and brewing terminology

items.prompt: Which heavily hopped beer style, originally brewed in England with extra hops to survive the long sea voyage to British troops stationed in South Asia, has become the flagship style of the American craft beer movement?
items.explanation:
Stands for India Pale Ale; originated in 18th-century England for export to British India
Extra hops acted as a preservative for the months-long sea voyage from Britain to the subcontinent
American craft brewers revived and transformed the style starting in the 1970s–1980s, using bold citrus- and pine-forward American hop varieties
Substyles include West Coast (dry, bitter), New England/Hazy (juicy, turbid), Double/Imperial (higher ABV, 7–10%+), and Session (lower ABV, ~4–5%)
Typical IBU range: 40–70 for standard versions; Double IPAs can exceed 90
The most-entered category at the Great American Beer Festival by number of competing beers
Sierra Nevada Pale Ale (1980) and Ballast Point Sculpin helped define the modern American hop-forward style
items.cardFront: India Pale Ale (hop-forward, craft beer staple)

items.prompt: Which dark, roasted-malt beer style, ranging from dry Irish versions to sweet milk and imperial Russian variations, takes its name from a word meaning 'strong' and was historically associated with the porters and laborers of 18th-century London?
items.explanation:
Originally called 'stout porter' — a stronger version of porter; the name eventually became its own category
Dry Irish stout (Guinness is the defining example): roasted barley gives coffee-like bitterness with a creamy, nitrogen-poured head
Milk stout adds lactose (milk sugar) for sweetness and body; oatmeal stout uses oats for a silky mouthfeel
Imperial/Russian Imperial stout: high-ABV (8–12%), originally brewed in Britain for export to the Russian imperial court in the 18th century
Pastry stouts (modern craft trend) add vanilla, chocolate, maple syrup, or other dessert-like adjuncts
Guinness, founded in Dublin in 1759, signed a 9,000-year lease on its St. James's Gate brewery
Stouts typically use highly kilned or roasted barley/malt, giving them their characteristic dark color and bitter, coffee-chocolate flavor

items.prompt: Which broad category of beer, fermented at cold temperatures with bottom-fermenting yeast (Saccharomyces pastorianus), accounts for roughly 90% of global beer production and originated in the caves and cellars of Bavaria?
items.explanation:
Name comes from German 'lagern' (to store); Bavarian brewers stored beer in cold caves for months to condition it
Uses bottom-fermenting yeast (Saccharomyces pastorianus) at 35–50°F (2–10°C), versus ales which use top-fermenting yeast at warmer temperatures
Accounts for roughly 90% of beer consumed worldwide; most mass-market commercial beers are lagers
Subcategories include pilsner, helles, bock, dunkel, schwarzbier, Märzen/Oktoberfest, and Vienna lager
The Reinheitsgebot (Bavarian Purity Law of 1516) originally permitted only water, barley, and hops — yeast was unknown at the time
Cold fermentation produces a cleaner, crisper flavor profile with fewer fruity esters than ales
Modern refrigeration in the 19th century allowed lager production year-round, fueling its global dominance

items.prompt: Which pale, golden, crisp beer style was first brewed in 1842 in a Bohemian city (now in the Czech Republic) using local Saaz hops, and became the model for nearly every mass-market pale beer in the world?
items.explanation:
First brewed in 1842 in Plzeň (Pilsen), Bohemia, by Bavarian brewer Josef Groll at the Burgess Brewery (now Pilsner Urquell)
Used pale malt (a recent innovation at the time), soft Bohemian water, and Saaz hops to create the world's first clear golden beer
Pilsner Urquell ('original source pilsner') remains in production; it is the original pilsner
Czech/Bohemian pilsner: more malty, slightly sweeter; German pilsner: drier, more bitter, crisper
Virtually every mass-market American and European pale lager (Budweiser, Miller, Heineken) descends from the pilsner style
Saaz hops (Czech noble hop) provide the floral, spicy aroma characteristic of the original style
Typical ABV: 4–5.5%; IBU: 25–45; always served cold in a tall, narrow glass to showcase clarity and carbonation
items.cardFront: Bohemian golden lager (1842, Plzeň, Saaz hops)

items.prompt: Which dark English beer style, first documented in 18th-century London, was so popular among street and river laborers that it became the first beer to be brewed on an industrial scale, and gave rise to a stronger variant that became its own style?
items.explanation:
First documented in London in the 1720s; named for the street and river porters (laborers) who favored it
Made with brown or chocolate malt; lighter and less roasty than its descendant, the stout
First beer brewed on a large industrial scale — London's massive porter breweries (Whitbread, Barclay Perkins) were among the largest factories in the world by the late 1700s
The stronger version, 'stout porter,' eventually became the separate 'stout' category
Nearly extinct by the mid-20th century; revived by craft brewers in the 1970s–1980s
Baltic porter (fermented with lager yeast, higher ABV) became popular across Scandinavia, Poland, and the Baltic states
Flavor profile: chocolate, caramel, toffee, mild roast; less bitter and lighter-bodied than most stouts
items.cardFront: Dark English beer (London 1720s, gave rise to stout)

items.prompt: Which style of beer, brewed with a large proportion of wheat (typically 50% or more of the grain bill), has German and Belgian varieties, often exhibiting banana and clove flavors from its distinctive yeast, and is traditionally served unfiltered and hazy?
items.explanation:
Brewed with a significant proportion of wheat alongside barley — typically 50–70% wheat in German versions
German Hefeweizen ('yeast wheat'): unfiltered, hazy, with banana (isoamyl acetate) and clove (4-vinylguaiacol) flavors from the yeast strain
Belgian witbier (white beer): spiced with coriander and orange peel; Hoegaarden is the defining example, revived by Pierre Celis in 1966
Kristallweizen: filtered version of Hefeweizen, clear rather than cloudy
American wheat ales use neutral yeast strains, producing a cleaner, less yeast-forward flavor
Berliner Weisse: tart, low-ABV (2.5–3.5%) German sour wheat beer, traditionally served with fruit syrup
Weihenstephaner (founded 1040, claimed oldest brewery in the world) and Erdinger are benchmark German wheat beer producers

items.prompt: Which amber- to copper-colored ale style, first brewed in England using coke-kilned malt that was lighter than traditional brown malt, bridges the gap between mild English bitters and the more aggressively hopped American versions that launched the craft beer revolution?
items.explanation:
First brewed in England in the early 1700s using coke-dried malt, which was lighter in color than wood-dried brown malt
English pale ale: balanced, malty, moderate bitterness; brands like Bass Pale Ale (1777) became globally famous
American pale ale: more hop-forward, using American hop varieties (Cascade, Centennial) for citrus and pine notes
Sierra Nevada Pale Ale (1980) defined the American pale ale style and remains one of the best-selling craft beers in the US
Historically called 'bitter' in English pubs; ESB (Extra Special Bitter) is a stronger substyle
Bridge between traditional English bitters and the more extreme bitterness of American IPAs
Typical ABV: 4–6%; IBU: 20–50; characterized by a balance of malt sweetness and hop bitterness

items.prompt: Which Belgian beer style, brewed near Brussels in the Pajottenland region, uses spontaneous fermentation by wild yeasts and bacteria (including Brettanomyces) instead of cultivated brewer's yeast, and must contain at least 30% unmalted wheat?
items.explanation:
Spontaneously fermented by wild yeasts and bacteria native to the Senne (Zenne) Valley near Brussels
Wort is cooled overnight in large, shallow open vessels called coolships, exposing it to airborne microbes including Brettanomyces bruxellensis and Brettanomyces lambicus
Must contain at least 30% unmalted wheat and use aged hops (for preservation, not flavor)
Gueuze: a blend of young (1-year) and old (2–3-year) lambics, bottle-conditioned like champagne
Kriek: lambic fermented with sour cherries; framboise uses raspberries
Key producers: Cantillon (Brussels), 3 Fonteinen (Beersel), Boon, Lindemans, Timmermans
Must be brewed within roughly 15 km of Brussels; fermentation traditionally occurs only in cooler months (October–April) when wild yeast activity is optimal

items.prompt: Which Irish brewery, founded in Dublin in 1759 with a famously long lease on its property, produces the world's best-known dry stout, served on nitrogen for its characteristic creamy head and cascading bubble effect?
items.explanation:
Founded by Arthur Guinness in 1759 at St. James's Gate, Dublin; he signed a 9,000-year lease at £45 per year
Dry Irish stout: roasted unmalted barley gives the signature bitter, coffee-like flavor and dark color
The 'surge and settle' cascade effect comes from nitrogen gas mixed with CO₂ (approximately 75% N₂, 25% CO₂) during pouring
The 'widget' (plastic ball inside cans) releases nitrogen when the can is opened, replicating draft pour texture
Guinness World Records book was created in 1955 by the company to settle pub arguments
Advertising icon: the toucan (created by artist John Gilroy in the 1930s); 'Guinness is good for you' was the early slogan
The brewery at St. James's Gate is the largest stout brewery in the world; Guinness is sold in over 120 countries

items.prompt: Which Dutch brewing company, founded in Amsterdam in 1864, became one of the world's largest beer exporters, recognizable by its green bottle and red star, and was among the first European lagers marketed successfully in the United States after Prohibition?
items.explanation:
Founded by Gerard Adriaan Heineken in Amsterdam in 1864 when he purchased the De Hooiberg brewery
The proprietary 'A-yeast' strain (developed in the 1880s by Dr. Elion, a student of Louis Pasteur) gives the beer its consistent flavor worldwide
Green bottle and red star branding are globally recognized; the brand uses a distinctive tilted 'e' in its logo sometimes called 'the smiling e'
One of the first European beers widely exported to the US; entered the American market immediately after Prohibition ended in 1933
Now the world's second-largest brewer by revenue (after AB InBev); owns over 300 brands including Amstel, Tiger, and Birra Moretti
Sponsor of the UEFA Champions League, Formula 1, and the James Bond franchise
Headquartered in Amsterdam; the original brewery is now the Heineken Experience museum

items.prompt: Which American lager, first brewed in St. Louis, Missouri in 1876 by Adolphus Busch using a recipe inspired by Bohemian beer, became 'The King of Beers' and the best-selling beer in the US for decades, while fighting a long trademark dispute with a Czech brewery from the city the name references?
items.explanation:
First brewed in 1876 by Adolphus Busch and Carl Conrad in St. Louis, Missouri, using a Bohemian-style recipe with rice as an adjunct for a lighter body
Named after Budweis (now České Budějovice), a Bohemian city with a centuries-old brewing tradition
Long trademark dispute with Budweiser Budvar (Budvar/Czechvar), a Czech brewery from the same city; both sell under different names in different markets
Brewed by Anheuser-Busch, now owned by AB InBev (Belgian-Brazilian conglomerate), the world's largest brewer
Beechwood aging: lager is conditioned on beechwood chips, which provide surface area for yeast — a process AB touts as distinctive
The Clydesdales (Budweiser's draft horse team) became an iconic advertising symbol, introduced in 1933 to celebrate the end of Prohibition
Bud Light (introduced 1982) eventually surpassed the original as America's best-selling beer

items.prompt: Which flowering plant of the species Humulus lupulus provides beer with bitterness, aroma, and natural preservative qualities, and is added at different stages of the brewing process to achieve different flavor effects?
items.explanation:
Humulus lupulus, a climbing bine (not vine) in the Cannabaceae family — related to cannabis
Female flower cones (strobili) contain lupulin glands that produce alpha acids (bittering) and essential oils (aroma)
Alpha acids isomerize during the boil to become iso-alpha acids, which provide bitterness measured in IBUs
Early additions (60+ minutes in the boil) extract bitterness; late additions and dry-hopping contribute aroma and flavor without significant bitterness
'Noble hops' are four traditional European varieties: Saaz (Czech), Hallertau, Tettnang, and Spalt (all German)
American varieties (Cascade, Citra, Mosaic, Simcoe) emphasize citrus, tropical fruit, and pine notes; drove the craft IPA movement
Before hops, brewers used 'gruit' (a mixture of herbs including yarrow, bog myrtle, and sweet gale) to flavor and preserve beer

items.prompt: Which brewing ingredient, made by soaking cereal grain (usually barley) in water to germinate and then kilning it to halt the process, provides the fermentable sugars that yeast converts to alcohol and CO2, and determines a beer's color and body?
items.explanation:
Made by soaking grain (steeping), allowing it to germinate to activate enzymes, then kiln-drying to stop growth and lock in flavor
Barley is the most common malting grain; wheat, rye, and oats are also used
During mashing, malt enzymes (amylase) convert starches into fermentable sugars (maltose, glucose) to create wort
Kilning temperature determines color and flavor: pale malt (lightly kilned), caramel/crystal malt (stewed), chocolate malt (highly roasted), black patent (almost burnt)
Provides the sugar (predominantly maltose) that yeast ferments into ethanol and CO₂
Lovibond or SRM (Standard Reference Method) scale measures malt color: pale lager ~2 SRM, porter ~30 SRM, stout ~40+ SRM
The Maillard reaction during kilning creates the caramel, toffee, biscuit, and roasty flavors found in different beer styles

items.prompt: What is the sweet, sugary liquid extracted from mashing malted grain in hot water called, which becomes beer only after boiling with hops and fermenting with yeast?
items.explanation:
Pronounced 'wert'; the unfermented liquid extracted during the mashing process
Created by steeping crushed malt in hot water (typically 148–158°F / 64–70°C) to activate enzymes that convert starch to sugar
Lautering separates the liquid wort from the spent grain; sparging rinses remaining sugars from the grain bed
The wort is then boiled for 60–90 minutes; hops are added during the boil for bitterness, flavor, and aroma
Original gravity (OG) measures the sugar concentration in wort before fermentation; higher OG = more potential alcohol
After boiling, wort is rapidly cooled and transferred to a fermenter where yeast is pitched (added)
The word derives from Old English 'wyrt' meaning plant or herb; cognate with German 'Würze' (spice/seasoning)

items.prompt: Which unit of measurement, developed by the American Society of Brewing Chemists, quantifies the bitterness in beer by measuring isomerized alpha acid concentration, with most beers falling between 5 and 100 on its scale?
items.explanation:
Stands for International Bitterness Units; standardized by the American Society of Brewing Chemists
Measures concentration of isomerized alpha acids (from hops) in parts per million via spectrophotometry
Typical ranges: light lager 5–15, wheat beer 10―20, pale ale 30–50, IPA 40–70, Double IPA 60–100+
Human taste threshold for detecting bitterness is roughly 4–9 IBU; above ~100 IBU, most palates cannot perceive additional bitterness
Perceived bitterness depends on malt sweetness, alcohol content, and residual sugar — a 70 IBU barleywine may taste less bitter than a 50 IBU pale ale
Measured by extracting hop compounds with organic solvent and measuring UV light absorption at 275 nm
Some craft brewers abandoned IBU labeling because the number alone does not predict how bitter a beer tastes
